name: Implement policy runner v1 scaffolding
description: Implement the first slice of the policy runner described in GitHub issue #74 and docs/policy-runner-design.md. Scope: add local policy config and evidence models, a minimal evaluator for guided/required rules, and an initial CLI surface for policy check/record/explain without MCP. Keep policy above adapters/storage and preserve current Python and adapter boundaries. Verification should include focused Rust tests for rule matching, decision evaluation, and config parsing.
result: Implemented the first policy-runner slice. Added core policy config/evidence/evaluation types in mnemix-core, CLI policy check/explain/record commands with file-backed policy.toml and policy-state.json handling, and Python client/model wrappers for the new policy surface. Verification: cargo test -p mnemix-core; cargo test -p mnemix-cli; python3 -m py_compile python/mnemix/models.py python/mnemix/client.py python/mnemix/__init__.py. Python pytest could not run because pytest is not installed in this environment.

commit.message: feat(policy): add initial policy runner surface

name: Write fixed-size embeddings for schema-v4 stores
description: Implement the next vector retrieval slice in the codex/vector-retrieval worktree. Scope: persist embeddings into the schema-v4 memory_embeddings fixed-size table on write and backfill, while keeping schema-v3 list-based persistence and retrieval behavior intact. Acceptance criteria: schema-v4 stores populate the fixed-size embedding table during auto-embed and backfill, vector status reflects fixed-size embedding readiness based on actual rows, existing schema-v3 stores continue to work unchanged, and tests cover schema-v4 write/backfill population.
result: Schema-v4 semantic retrieval now reads embeddings from the fixed-size memory_embeddings table and bulk-loads memory payloads by id from memories, while schema-v3 stores continue to use the legacy List<Float32> embedding column. Also removed stale fixed-size embedding rows during delete_memory to keep semantic candidates consistent. Added regression coverage proving v4 semantic search still works after clearing the legacy list embedding and v3 semantic search still falls back to the list-based path. Verification: cargo fmt --all; cargo test -p mnemix-lancedb --lib semantic_only_search_uses_fixed_size_embeddings_on_v4_store; cargo test -p mnemix-lancedb --lib semantic_only_search_falls_back_to_v3_list_embeddings; cargo test -p mnemix-lancedb --lib -p mnemix-cli --test cli_flow; cargo test -p mnemix-core -p mnemix-lancedb -p mnemix-cli

name: Scaffold workspace crates
description: Create the initial crate skeletons for Milestone 0. Scope: add placeholder crates for mnemix-core, mnemix-lancedb, mnemix-cli, and mnemix-test-support, with minimal Cargo manifests, lib/main files, and module placeholders aligned with docs/mnemix-roadmap.md. Acceptance criteria: the workspace builds with placeholder crates, crate responsibilities are separated correctly, and no storage-specific details leak into the core crate structure prematurely.
result: Scaffolded the initial workspace crates and supporting directories. Added placeholder crates for mnemix-core, mnemix-lancedb, mnemix-cli, mnemix-types, and mnemix-test-support with minimal compiling source files and cargo manifests. Also added placeholder Python, adapter, example, test, and docs scaffolds required by the roadmap. Key decisions: keep placeholder code compiling cleanly, keep the core crate storage-agnostic, and isolate the LanceDB placeholder into its own crate. Verification: workspace members resolve correctly and the placeholder crates compile under the milestone 0 validation commands.

name: Add vector status and index readiness reporting
description: Implement the next vector retrieval slice in the codex/vector-retrieval worktree. Scope: add backend and CLI reporting for vector readiness, embedding coverage, and vector-index availability so the current List<Float32> embedding schema has an explicit operational status while semantic retrieval remains functional. Acceptance criteria: LanceDB backend exposes a typed vector status snapshot, vectors show surfaces readiness/coverage information, status distinguishes semantic retrieval availability from vector-index availability, and tests cover the reported values for disabled stores, partially embedded stores, and provider-backed vector-enabled stores.

name: Plan optional LanceDB vector retrieval layer
description:
# Implementation Plan: Optional LanceDB Vector Retrieval Layer

**Dex task:** `3zywsfb4`
**GitHub issue:** `#75`
**Date:** March 17, 2026

## Summary

Add LanceDB vector capabilities to Mnemix as an additive, optional retrieval layer that improves semantic recall without replacing the current text-first, explainable retrieval model. Keep `mnemix-core` storage-agnostic, place all LanceDB and embedding mechanics behind `mnemix-lancedb`, and preserve graceful fallback to the current lexical and metadata-filtered search flows when vector support is disabled or unavailable.

## Why This Fits Mnemix

Mnemix is explicitly local-first, structured-memory-first, and text-first today. The current product plan and LanceDB research already point toward optional vector retrieval as a later layer rather than a redesign:

- `docs/mnemix-plan-v3.md` defines retrieval as text-first, practical, and explainable.
- `docs/lancedb-rust-sdk-agent-guide.md` calls out full-text and vector retrieval in one stack.
- `mnemix-lancedb` already owns backend indexing and storage mechanics, which is the correct boundary for vector index and embedding plumbing.

The value of adding vector retrieval is not generic “AI database” marketing. The concrete Mnemix benefit is better memory recall when query wording differs from stored memory wording, especially for summaries, preferences, procedures, and other distilled memories where lexical overlap is often weak.

## Product Goals

1. Improve recall when a query is semantically related to a stored memory but does not share enough exact words for FTS alone.
2. Keep lexical retrieval, metadata filters, and explainability as first-class behaviors.
3. Make vector support optional per store and per query.
4. Avoid introducing a mandatory cloud embedding dependency.
5. Preserve older stores and non-vector stores without regressions.
6. Keep Python as a wrapper surface, not a second implementation.

## Non-Goals

- replacing the current progressive-disclosure recall model
- making semantic search the default on day one
- requiring external API-hosted embeddings
- leaking LanceDB vector or Arrow schema details into `mnemix-core`
- building a broad UI workflow around vector features
- introducing branch-specific vector workflows in v1

## Current State

### Existing product contracts

- `RecallQuery` supports scope, text hint, limit, and disclosure depth.
- `SearchQuery` is currently text-only.
- `RecallResult` already carries explanation metadata and layered results.
- backend capability flags exist for search, history, restore, checkpoints, optimize, branches, and clones, but not for semantic or hybrid retrieval.

### Existing storage model

- `MemoryRecord` stores `title`, `summary`, `detail`, and composed `fts_text`.
- `mnemix-lancedb` already creates and maintains an FTS index on `fts_text`.
- schema versioning already exists through `SCHEMA_VERSION` and `schema_metadata`.
- the backend already has explicit optimization and checkpoint flows that can be reused for embedding backfill and reindex operations.

### Current constraints

- retrieval is intentionally text-first today
- there is no embedding field in the product model
- there is no embedding provider abstraction yet
- there is no semantic ranking explanation type yet

## Scope Analysis

### Affected Areas

| Area | Changes Required |
|------|------------------|
| `mnemix-core` query model | Add product-level retrieval mode and semantic match explanation semantics |
| `mnemix-core` backend capabilities | Add capability flags for semantic and hybrid retrieval |
| `mnemix-lancedb` schema | Add optional embedding storage columns and store config metadata |
| `mnemix-lancedb` indexing | Add vector index management alongside current FTS index management |
| `mnemix-lancedb` query execution | Add semantic candidate generation and hybrid ranking |
| `mnemix-lancedb` maintenance | Add embedding backfill and vector reindex flows |
| CLI | Add mode flags, config surface, provenance output, and operational commands |
| Python wrapper | Mirror the new query and capability contracts |
| Tests | Add deterministic semantic ranking, migration, fallback, and index lifecycle coverage |
| Docs | Add architecture, operations, and user-facing usage guidance |

### Affected Layers

- [x] Core domain query semantics
- [x] Backend capability surface
- [x] LanceDB schema and index management
- [x] Backend ranking and retrieval execution
- [x] CLI surface
- [x] Python wrapper surface
- [x] Tests and benchmarks
- [x] Documentation
- [ ] Web or service APIs
- [ ] Compliance workflows

## Design Principles

### 1. Text-first remains the default model

The existing retrieval model in Mnemix should remain valid and useful without vectors. Semantic retrieval is an additive enhancement for candidate recall and ranking, not a replacement for FTS, metadata filters, pinning, or progressive disclosure.

### 2. Storage details stay inside `mnemix-lancedb`

`mnemix-core` should express product semantics such as retrieval mode, semantic match provenance, and capability detection. It must not expose LanceDB vector column details, ANN index types, embedding model implementation details, or Arrow-specific schema shapes.

### 3. Optionality exists at both store and query levels

- A store can operate with vectors disabled.
- A query can request lexical-only behavior even on a vector-enabled store.
- Hybrid behavior should degrade safely when vector support is unavailable.

### 4. Explainability remains a product requirement

Mnemix should continue to expose why a memory was surfaced. Hybrid and semantic retrieval must surface explanation metadata such as lexical match, semantic match, hybrid match, pinning, recency, and importance boosts.

### 5. Migration must be safe and reversible

Existing stores must stay readable. Enabling vectors, backfilling embeddings, and rebuilding vector indexes should create or support checkpoints so users can inspect and recover prior state if something goes wrong.

## Proposed Product-Level API Changes

### Retrieval mode

Add a new product-level retrieval mode enum in `mnemix-core`, for example:

- `LexicalOnly`
- `SemanticOnly`
- `Hybrid`

Initial default recommendation:

- `SearchQuery`: `LexicalOnly`
- `RecallQuery`: `LexicalOnly`

Future default recommendation once proven:

- `RecallQuery`: possibly `Hybrid`
- `SearchQuery`: still caller-chosen

### `SearchQuery`

Extend `SearchQuery` to support:

- `mode: RetrievalMode`
- optional future threshold fields if needed

Keep the query text required. Do not introduce provider- or embedding-specific knobs at the product level.

### `RecallQuery`

Extend `RecallQuery` to support:

- `retrieval_mode: RetrievalMode`

Reuse the current `text` field as the semantic query text when present. Do not add a second semantic-only text field unless practical use forces a split later.

### Explanation metadata

Extend retrieval explanations to distinguish:

- lexical match
- semantic match
- hybrid match

Possible new explanation reasons:

- `LexicalMatch`
- `SemanticMatch`
- `HybridMatch`

The existing ranking reasons for pinning, importance, recency, and archival expansion should remain intact.

### Backend capability flags

Add new backend capabilities in `mnemix-core`:

- `SemanticSearch`
- `HybridSearch`

This allows CLI and Python clients to inspect feature support without relying on backend-specific knowledge.

## Proposed Storage and Schema Changes

All vector and embedding storage changes belong in `mnemix-lancedb`.

### Memory table additions

Add nullable vector-related columns to the `memories` table, such as:

- `embedding_model` (`Utf8`, nullable)
- `embedding_dim` (`UInt32`, nullable)
- `embedding_updated_at` (`UInt64`, nullable)
- `embedding` (vector column, exact Arrow shape determined by LanceDB requirements)

Key rules:

- embeddings must be optional
- rows without embeddings must remain searchable lexically
- `payload_json` remains the durable round-trip source of truth

### Store metadata

Persist vector configuration in schema metadata or a dedicated config table:

- vectors enabled or disabled
- configured model identifier
- expected embedding dimensions
- optional auto-embed-on-write behavior

This avoids mixed-dimension data and enables explicit validation during open, backfill, and reindex flows.

### Schema evolution

Bump `SCHEMA_VERSION` and define an explicit migration story:

- new stores create vector-capable schema when vectors are enabled
- existing stores open without forced migration
- enabling vectors on an older store triggers an explicit migration or feature-enablement flow

## Embedding Strategy

### Provider abstraction

Introduce an embedding abstraction owned outside `mnemix-core`, ideally in `mnemix-lancedb` or a small adapter module used by it:

- `embed_text(&self, text: &str) -> Result<Vec<f32>, Error>`
- `model_id(&self) -> &str`
- `dimensions(&self) -> usize`

This trait should remain backend-facing and must not leak into the stable core query contracts.

### Initial provider policy

Recommended v1 support:

- disabled or no-op provider
- deterministic fake provider for tests
- optional local model provider later

Do not make a hosted embedding API the baseline requirement.

### Embedding input composition

Create a backend-side semantic input text, parallel to `fts_text`, composed from:

- title
- summary
- detail
- selected tags
- selected entities

Do not embed raw `payload_json`. Keep the semantic input targeted and readable.

### Re-embedding policy

Recompute embeddings when:

- a memory is first written and vectors are enabled
- fields that affect retrieval text change
- the configured embedding model changes

Mark stale embeddings and provide explicit maintenance flows for backfill and model migration.

## Retrieval and Ranking Design

### Candidate generation

Implement retrieval in two stages:

1. generate lexical candidates using the existing FTS and filter flow
2. generate semantic candidates using vector similarity when available

Then merge the candidate sets in product code within the backend.

### Hybrid ranking

Start with a simple, inspectable ranking model:

- lexical match bonus
- semantic similarity contribution
- pinning priority for recall
- importance boost
- recency boost
- scope filter as a hard constraint where possible

Avoid opaque learned ranking in the first implementation.

### Recall behavior

Preserve the current progressive disclosure structure:

1. pinned context
2. summary layer
3. archival layer

Vectors should improve candidate selection within the summary and archival layers. Pinning should remain rule-based and bounded by product semantics rather than ANN score alone.

### Search behavior

Allow the caller to request:

- lexical-only search for today’s behavior
- semantic-only search for experiments or advanced workflows
- hybrid search for best-effort recall plus precision

## Indexing Plan

### Existing index behavior

Keep the current FTS index lifecycle intact. It remains the primary lexical retrieval index.

### Vector index lifecycle

Add vector index inspection and creation in `mnemix-lancedb`, parallel to the existing FTS index helper:

- inspect existing indexes
- create vector index if missing
- make creation idempotent

Start with one backend-chosen default vector index family rather than exposing multiple strategies in the product surface immediately.

### Maintenance implications

Treat vector indexes as explicit operational assets:

- stats should report whether a vector index exists
- optimize flows should be able to reindex or refresh vector structures
- docs should note that vector freshness and index optimization are operational concerns, not invisible guarantees

## Configuration and Fallback Policy

### Store-level configuration

Add backend configuration inputs such as:

- `vectors_enabled`
- `embedding_model_id`
- `auto_embed_on_write`

Persist the resulting chosen config as store metadata.

### Query-time fallback

Recommended behavior:

- `LexicalOnly`: always succeeds using current behavior
- `Hybrid`: falls back to lexical if vectors are unavailable, while preserving explainability
- `SemanticOnly`: returns a typed capability error if semantic retrieval is unavailable

This keeps the common path resilient while making strict semantic-only requests explicit.

## CLI and Python Surface Changes

### CLI

Add commands and flags incrementally:

- `search --mode lexical|semantic|hybrid`
- `recall --mode lexical|semantic|hybrid`
- `init --vectors enabled|disabled`
- `optimize --reindex-vectors`
- `embeddings backfill`

Human-readable output should include match provenance such as:

- `matched: lexical`
- `matched: semantic`
- `matched: hybrid`

### Python wrapper

Mirror the Rust product contracts:

- retrieval mode enum
- capability inspection
- vector-enablement and maintenance calls where exposed by CLI or backend APIs

Do not add Python-only retrieval semantics.

## Migration and Backfill Plan

### Existing store handling

Older stores without vector columns must continue to open and support lexical search and recall.

### Enabling vectors on an existing store

Provide an explicit enablement path:

1. validate current store state
2. create a checkpoint if policy requires or recommends one
3. add vector-capable schema state
4. backfill embeddings in batches
5. create or refresh vector index

### Backfill behavior

Backfill should be:

- resumable
- idempotent
- batch-oriented
- safe on partially embedded stores

If a run fails partway through, lexical behavior must remain intact.

## Testing and Verification Plan

Per project conventions, finish substantive implementation work with `./scripts/check.sh`. For this feature, add targeted tests before enabling the feature by default.

### `mnemix-core` tests

- retrieval mode validation
- explanation serialization and equality behavior
- capability flag behavior

### `mnemix-lancedb` tests

- initialize store with vectors disabled
- initialize store with vectors enabled
- open legacy store without vectors
- remember writes embeddings when configured
- rows without embeddings still work in lexical mode
- hybrid candidate merge is deterministic
- recall layering still respects pinned, summary, and archival semantics
- vector index creation is idempotent
- backfill updates missing or stale embeddings only
- model mismatch is rejected cleanly

### CLI and Python tests

- query mode flag parsing
- fallback behavior
- capability inspection output
- maintenance command coverage

### Benchmark and evaluation fixtures

Add a curated semantic relevance fixture set containing:

- lexical-overlap cases
- semantic-only match cases
- near-duplicate memories
- mixed pinned and archival cases

Use this to validate that hybrid retrieval improves recall without breaking current ranking semantics.

## Operational Considerations

### Stats and observability

Extend stats to report:

- total memories
- embedded memories
- embedding coverage percentage
- current embedding model identifier
- vector index presence

### Maintenance safety

Create or recommend checkpoints before:

- enabling vectors
- bulk backfill
- model migration
- vector reindex

### Failure modes

Account for:

- missing provider configuration
- dimension mismatch
- partially embedded stores
- stale embeddings after model changes
- vector index not yet built

Each should either degrade cleanly to lexical behavior or return a typed, inspectable error.

## Phased Rollout

### Phase 0: Contracts and design freeze

- define retrieval mode semantics in `mnemix-core`
- define explanation extensions
- define backend capability extensions
- define store-level fallback policy

Acceptance criteria:

- storage-specific details remain out of `mnemix-core`
- contracts are reviewable and documented

### Phase 1: Schema and config scaffolding

- add vector-related nullable columns
- add store metadata for vector configuration
- bump schema version and migration handling

Acceptance criteria:

- new and existing stores both open successfully
- lexical retrieval remains unaffected

### Phase 2: Embedding pipeline

- add embedding provider abstraction
- add deterministic fake provider for tests
- support embed-on-write
- support explicit backfill

Acceptance criteria:

- vector-enabled stores can persist embeddings
- backfill is resumable and safe

### Phase 3: Semantic and hybrid retrieval

- add semantic candidate generation
- add hybrid ranking
- add explanation provenance
- preserve progressive disclosure semantics

Acceptance criteria:

- curated semantic test cases show improved recall
- pinned and summary behaviors do not regress

### Phase 4: CLI and Python integration

- expose query modes
- expose vector configuration and maintenance commands
- mirror capability inspection in Python
- document expected usage

Acceptance criteria:

- end-to-end lexical, semantic, and hybrid flows are usable from CLI and Python

### Phase 5: Hardening and operationalization

- benchmark default index choice
- extend stats and optimize flows
- refine ranking based on test fixtures
- document maintenance guidance

Acceptance criteria:

- stable defaults exist
- operational guidance is documented
- verification commands are recorded and reproducible

## Risks

- semantic scores could overpower the current importance, recency, and pinning semantics if not bounded carefully
- schema evolution could become painful if embeddings are made mandatory too early
- vector index maintenance may add complexity that does not pay off for very small stores
- hosted embedding dependencies would conflict with the project’s local-first posture if adopted prematurely
- opaque ranking would weaken the current explainability goal

## Recommendation

Implement vector retrieval as an opt-in backend capability that improves candidate recall and supports hybrid ranking, while preserving the current text-first product posture and explainable layered recall model. The first milestone should be contract and schema work, not model-provider integration. In practice, the right v1 is “hybrid-capable but still lexical-first by default.”

## Suggested Execution Backlog

1. Add retrieval mode and semantic capability contracts in `mnemix-core`.
2. Add vector-capable schema and store metadata handling in `mnemix-lancedb`.
3. Add embedding provider abstraction and deterministic fake embedder.
4. Implement embed-on-write and backfill flows.
5. Implement semantic candidate generation and hybrid ranking.
6. Extend recall and search explanations for semantic provenance.
7. Expose the feature in CLI and Python surfaces.
8. Add migration, operational stats, and maintenance support.
9. Validate with semantic relevance fixtures and full repo checks.

## Acceptance Criteria

- vector support is optional and additive
- non-vector stores continue to work without behavioral regression
- semantic and hybrid retrieval are expressed as product-level semantics, not backend leaks
- explainability remains visible in search and recall results
- existing progressive disclosure and pinning behavior remain intact
- migration and backfill flows are safe and checkpoint-aware
- CLI and Python wrappers expose the feature consistently

## Verification Plan For This Planning Task

1. write the implementation plan to `.plans/lancedb-vector-retrieval-implementation-plan.md`
2. create a Dex task that tracks the planning work
3. sync the task to GitHub as a repository issue
4. update the Dex task and GitHub issue with the final detailed markdown plan
5. record verification in the Dex task result when complete
result: Created the optional LanceDB vector retrieval implementation plan and published it across the repo, Dex, and GitHub. Deliverables: added .plans/lancedb-vector-retrieval-implementation-plan.md with a detailed phased plan covering product contracts, schema/index changes, embedding boundaries, hybrid ranking, migration, CLI/Python surface, testing, operational concerns, risks, and acceptance criteria; updated Dex task 3zywsfb4 description to the full markdown plan; updated GitHub issue #75 to the same body content. Verification: confirmed the plan file exists locally, ran dex show 3zywsfb4 --full to verify the full task description, and ran gh issue view 75 --json number,title,body,url to verify the GitHub issue title, URL, and full markdown body.

name: Add CI and helper scripts
description: Create the initial engineering automation for Milestone 0. Scope: add GitHub workflow files for fmt/clippy/test/doc checks, plus minimal helper scripts if useful for local verification. Keep CI aligned with the roadmap and Rust best practices: formatting, linting, testing, and documentation generation from day one. Acceptance criteria: CI files exist, commands map to the workspace layout, and the automation is suitable for validating the empty scaffold before feature work begins.
result: Added the initial engineering automation. Created GitHub workflow files for CI, docs presence checks, and release-preparation verification, plus scripts/check.sh and scripts/release.sh. Key decisions: make fmt, clippy, test, doc, and cargo-deny part of the baseline from day one; keep release automation conservative and verification-oriented at this stage. Verification: workflow files were created against the actual workspace layout and helper scripts were made executable.

name: Add root workspace config
description: Implement the root engineering baseline for Milestone 0. Scope: create root Cargo workspace metadata, rust-toolchain pinning, rustfmt/clippy configuration, deny/license policy, editor config, gitignore, README/CHANGELOG placeholders, and any root-level policy files needed by the roadmap. Follow Rust API guideline metadata expectations and Rust best-practice linting discipline. Acceptance criteria: root files exist, workspace metadata is coherent, lint/format policy is explicit, and root docs/policies are ready for CI and crate scaffolding.
result: Added the root workspace baseline: Cargo.toml workspace metadata, rust-toolchain.toml, rustfmt.toml, clippy.toml, deny.toml, .editorconfig, .gitignore, README.md, CHANGELOG.md, and LICENSE. Key decisions: use Rust 2024 edition, shared workspace package metadata, strict but practical lint defaults, and tracked Dex project instructions via AGENTS.md. Verification: root files are present, cargo-deny configuration is valid, and the workspace metadata matches the repo scaffold and roadmap expectations.

name: Prepare v0.2.0 release checklist and version bump
description: Prepare the next Mnemix release after the rebrand. Scope: bump the workspace and Python package version to 0.2.0, replace the one-off PyPI release plan with a reusable release checklist document, run the local Python package release preflight, and open a PR from main. Acceptance criteria: version sources are aligned, docs/release-checklist.md documents the generic recurring release procedure, release-related references point at the new checklist, and ./scripts/check-python-package.sh passes.
result: Prepared the v0.2.0 release update on PR #29. Aligned the Python and Rust workspace versions to 0.2.0, replaced docs/pypi-release-plan.md with the reusable docs/release-checklist.md runbook, and updated README and CHANGELOG references. Verification completed locally: python/.release-venv/bin/python -m pytest python/tests, python/.release-venv/bin/python -m build --sdist, python/.release-venv/bin/python -m twine check --strict python/dist/*, cargo build -p mnemix-cli, and PYTHON_BIN=$PWD/python/.release-venv/bin/python ./scripts/check-python-bundled-wheel.sh all passed.

name: Milestone 6: Python binding and first adapter
description: Implement Milestone 6 from docs/mnemix-roadmap.md. Scope: create the Python package scaffold, stable high-level Python entry points, serialization-safe request and response wrappers, an initial AI DX Toolkit adapter proof of concept, and usage examples with binding tests. Keep Python as a wrapper around Rust core behavior rather than a second implementation. Acceptance criteria: Python wraps Rust behavior without duplicating core logic, the adapter uses only public APIs, and core semantics stay aligned across CLI and Python surfaces.
result: Reconciled during post-merge cleanup: Milestone 6 had already landed on main and is marked complete so only the active bundled-wheel task remains open.

name: Define workspace scaffold
description: Break down Milestone 0 into a concrete repository structure aligned with docs/mnemix-roadmap.md. Scope: confirm the top-level workspace layout, root files, crate boundaries, test-support placement, docs placement, examples layout, and scripts folder. Reference docs/mnemix-plan-v3.md, docs/mnemix-roadmap.md, docs/lancedb-rust-sdk-agent-guide.md, docs/coding-guidlines/rust-api-guidelines/checklist.md, and docs/coding-guidlines/rust-best-practices/README.md. Acceptance criteria: the intended scaffold is explicit enough to implement without redesign during file creation, crate boundaries are clear, and the layout stays Rust-workspace-first and local-first.
result: Defined the initial repository scaffold and documented it in docs/repo-scaffold-spec.md. Locked the top-level workspace shape, crate boundaries, placeholder policy, and non-Rust scaffold directories so milestone 0 implementation could proceed without repo redesign. Key decisions: keep a Rust-workspace-first layout, include mnemix-types from the start, and scaffold Python/adapters/examples early without giving them implementation ownership. Verification: scaffold spec written and aligned with docs/mnemix-roadmap.md, docs/mnemix-plan-v3.md, and docs/lancedb-rust-sdk-agent-guide.md.

name: Bundle CLI in platform-specific PyPI wheels
description: Prepare Mnemix for a lower-friction Python install by publishing platform-specific PyPI wheels that bundle the Rust mnemix CLI binary. Scope: define supported target platforms, update Python packaging to include bundled binaries, prefer bundled binary lookup at runtime, expand GitHub Actions to build/test/publish wheels per platform, and document fallback behavior for unsupported platforms. Acceptance criteria: pip install mnemix works without a separate CLI install on supported platforms; MNEMIX_BINARY and PATH fallback still work; trusted publishing uploads per-platform wheels plus sdist; docs reflect the new installation model.
result: Bundled platform-specific Python wheels merged on main in PR #25. The Python package now prefers a bundled CLI binary on supported platforms while keeping MNEMIX_BINARY and PATH fallback behavior. Verification: hosted validation passed across Linux, macOS, and Windows, and issue #24 was closed after merge.

name: Milestone 1: Core domain contract freeze
description: Implement Milestone 1 from docs/mnemix-roadmap.md. Scope: define the product model independent of storage, including typed domain IDs, value objects, memory records, recall/search/history/stats query types, checkpoint and retention types, backend capability traits, crate-level docs, and unit tests for domain invariants. Follow docs/mnemix-plan-v3.md, docs/lancedb-rust-sdk-agent-guide.md, and docs/mnemix-coding-guidelines.md. Acceptance criteria: no lancedb dependency in the core crate, public APIs use typed parameters rather than ambiguous flags, public error types are typed and documented, and docs/examples demonstrate a coherent end-to-end core flow.
result: Reconciled during post-merge cleanup: Milestone 1 was already merged and is marked complete so the Dex board reflects the actual repo state.

name: Rebrand Temporal Plane to Mnemix
description: Rebrand the project from Temporal Plane to Mnemix across the repo's public surfaces, package names, CLI names, crate names, docs, examples, workflows, and AI context. Acceptance criteria: README/docs use Mnemix and mnemix, the CLI/package/import/crate names are updated coherently, and validation passes for the fully renamed public surfaces with no remaining Temporal Plane identifiers in repo-owned paths or content.
result: Completed the full project rebrand from Temporal Plane to Mnemix. Renamed the Rust workspace crates, CLI binary, Python package and imports, adapter module, canonical docs, examples, workflows, AGENTS/instruction files, AI context, and repository metadata. Removed Temporal Plane identifiers from repo-owned paths and content, updated the local git remote to the renamed GitHub repository, and validated the result with the full Python test suite, adapter tests, and ./scripts/check.sh.

name: Implement vector retrieval contracts
description: Start the first implementation slice for optional LanceDB vector retrieval in Mnemix. Scope: add product-level retrieval mode semantics and backend capability flags in mnemix-core, update existing call sites and tests, and keep all storage-specific details out of the core crate. Acceptance criteria: mnemix-core exposes typed retrieval-mode and semantic capability contracts, the workspace compiles with those changes, and the backend can advertise the new capabilities without yet implementing vector search behavior.
result: Implemented the first vector-retrieval contract slice without changing existing lexical behavior. Changes: added RetrievalMode to mnemix-core with lexical-only defaults for RecallQuery and SearchQuery plus explicit accessors and builders; added SemanticSearch and HybridSearch backend capability flags and helper methods; re-exported RetrievalMode from mnemix-core; extended recall reason string mappings for future semantic and hybrid provenance in the CLI and Python typed models. Key decision: keep constructors backward-compatible so current CLI, backend, and wrapper flows remain lexical-first until later storage and ranking work lands. Verification: cargo fmt --all; cargo test -p mnemix-core -p mnemix-lancedb -p mnemix-cli all passed. Python verification was attempted with python3 -m pytest python/tests/test_client.py but pytest is not installed in the current environment.

name: Add fixed-size embedding schema groundwork
description: Implement the next vector retrieval slice in the codex/vector-retrieval worktree. Scope: add schema and backend groundwork for a vector-indexable embedding shape for new stores while preserving compatibility for existing schema-v3 stores. Acceptance criteria: backend distinguishes indexable embedding storage readiness by schema version, new stores initialize with the new embedding schema, old stores remain readable and writable, and tests cover schema versioning and vector status behavior across legacy, current, and new stores.
result: Added schema-v4 groundwork for a future LanceDB-native vector index path. New stores now initialize at schema version 4, vector enablement on schema-v4 stores creates a dedicated fixed-size embedding table for future indexable storage, schema-v3 stores remain readable and writable on the existing list-based path, and vector status now distinguishes persisted embedding storage from indexable embedding storage. Verified with cargo fmt --all, cargo test -p mnemix-lancedb --lib -p mnemix-cli --test cli_flow, and cargo test -p mnemix-core -p mnemix-lancedb -p mnemix-cli.

name: Milestone 0: Workspace and engineering baseline
description: Implement the roadmap's Milestone 0 for Mnemix. Scope: create the Rust workspace skeleton, root Cargo metadata, toolchain/lint/format config, CI baseline, crate stubs, and initial docs scaffolding. Follow docs/mnemix-roadmap.md, docs/mnemix-plan-v3.md, docs/lancedb-rust-sdk-agent-guide.md, and docs/coding-guidlines Rust guidance. Acceptance criteria: workspace builds, fmt/clippy/test/doc checks are wired, placeholder crates exist, and repo metadata files are present.
result: Completed Milestone 0 by establishing the full repository baseline for Mnemix. Changes: defined the scaffold in docs/repo-scaffold-spec.md; added the root workspace metadata and policy files; scaffolded the core, LanceDB backend, CLI, shared types, and test-support crates; added placeholder Python, adapter, example, test, and docs directories; and added CI plus helper scripts. Key decisions: keep Rust as the source-of-truth workspace, keep mnemix-core free of storage concerns, include mnemix-types from the start, and wire verification tooling into the repo before feature work. Verification: cargo fmt, cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings, cargo test --workspace, cargo doc --workspace --no-deps, and cargo deny check all passed on the new scaffold.

name: Milestone 7: Advanced storage workflows
description: Implement Milestone 7 from docs/mnemix-roadmap.md. Scope: prepare advanced branch-aware storage workflows, evaluate shallow and deep clone support, add advanced import staging concepts, prototype branch experiments, and document branch lifecycle semantics. Follow docs/lancedb-rust-sdk-agent-guide.md by using lance selectively where lancedb stops being the right abstraction. Acceptance criteria: no breaking changes to the v1 product model, advanced functionality remains clearly marked as advanced, and internal abstractions can support alternate timelines safely.
result: Implemented Milestone 7 advanced storage workflows in the M7 worktree. Delivered branch domain types and advanced storage traits in mnemix-core; Lance-backed branch create/list/delete, staged import, shallow clone, deep clone, and backend coverage in mnemix-lancedb; the public branch experiment example at crates/mnemix-lancedb/examples/branch-experiment.rs plus examples/branch-experiment/README.md; and branch lifecycle documentation linked from the main architecture docs. Verification: editor diagnostics are clean in the M7 worktree. Full cargo validation was not run because cargo is not currently available in the terminal environment on this machine.

name: Add embedding provider plumbing
description: Implement the next vector retrieval slice in the codex/vector-retrieval worktree. Scope: add a backend-side embedding provider abstraction and explicit vector settings surface in mnemix-lancedb, wire the vector_config table into backend state, and keep semantic retrieval behavior disabled by default. Acceptance criteria: the backend can expose vector settings cleanly, disabled/default behavior is explicit and test-covered, and no storage-specific provider details leak into mnemix-core.
result: Implemented the embedding-provider plumbing slice in the codex/vector-retrieval worktree. Changes: added a backend-owned EmbeddingProvider trait and EmbeddingProviderError type in mnemix-lancedb; added public VectorSettings and LanceDbOpenOptions types; extended LanceDbBackend to load and expose effective vector settings, report provider availability, and compute whether auto-embed-on-write is currently possible; added runtime validation for invalid vector settings and provider/config mismatches; exported the new backend-facing types from the crate root. Tests: replaced internal vector-config assertions with public vector-settings checks and added coverage for matching provider runtime capability, provider model mismatch, provider dimension mismatch, and invalid auto-embed configuration. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check; cargo test -p mnemix-core -p mnemix-lancedb -p mnemix-cli; cargo test -p mnemix-lancedb --lib backend::tests::open_with_matching_embedding_provider_reports_runtime_capability all passed.

name: Milestone 5: Version-aware safety features
description: Implement Milestone 5 from docs/mnemix-roadmap.md. Scope: add historical inspection APIs and commands, restore flow, pre-import and pre-optimize checkpoint policy, retention configuration types, optimize command behavior, tag and checkpoint naming policy, and tests for restore and recovery behavior. Preserve the distinction between checkout and restore from docs/mnemix-plan-v3.md and docs/lancedb-rust-sdk-agent-guide.md. Acceptance criteria: restore creates a new current state rather than mutating history semantics, cleanup is conservative by default, tagged versions are protected from routine cleanup, and users can inspect history before destructive operations.
result: Reconciled during post-merge cleanup: Milestone 5 had already landed and is marked complete to match the merged workspace history.

name: Milestone 2: Local LanceDB backend MVP
description: Implement Milestone 2 from docs/mnemix-roadmap.md. Scope: build the first persistent local backend using LanceDB for connect/open/init flows, table creation, schema version metadata, remember/search/show primitives, FTS indexing, filtering, ranking integration, version listing, checkpoint tag creation, and initial import/export skeletons. Use docs/lancedb-rust-sdk-agent-guide.md for the lancedb vs lance boundary and keep versioning/tags in the MVP. Acceptance criteria: a local store can be initialized, memory records can be persisted and searched by FTS plus scope filters, checkpoints can be created and listed, integration tests cover create/open/add/delete/query flows, and backend details remain hidden behind core traits.
result: Reconciled during post-merge cleanup: Milestone 2 was already merged and is marked complete so dependent milestones reflect reality.

name: Design policy runner for guided and required memory workflows
description:
## Objective
Design a configurable policy runner that supports two memory modes for agent hosts:

- `guided`: recommend recall/writeback based on workflow context
- `required`: enforce memory actions for configured scenarios such as `git commit`, PR creation, release prep, or risky migrations

The policy runner should sit above the Mnemix adapters and determine when recall, writeback, checkpoint creation, or an explicit skip reason is required.

## Why This Matters
Today Mnemix supports intelligent, guided memory usage through adapters and instructions. Some teams will also want stronger guarantees for specific workflow checkpoints. The policy runner should make those checkpoints configurable without turning all memory usage into mandatory busywork.

## Scope
This task is planning and design only.

In scope:
- define the policy runner architecture
- define the rule model and evidence model
- define trigger points and enforcement points
- define a config format for guided vs required rules
- define a CLI and adapter-facing API surface
- define how this could later be exposed via MCP
- define a phased implementation plan

Out of scope:
- implementing the policy runner
- implementing git hooks
- implementing an MCP server
- adding new storage schema unless the design proves it is necessary

## Product Goals
The design should satisfy these goals:

1. Keep the base Mnemix client and storage semantics generic.
2. Put workflow enforcement and judgment in a policy layer, not in storage.
3. Support both soft guidance and hard gating.
4. Allow explicit skip paths so users are not forced to store low-signal memory.
5. Make policy decisions explainable and inspectable.
6. Keep the design usable from coding agents first, then generalize to other hosts.

## Core Design Questions
The plan should answer:

1. What triggers should the policy runner support in v1?
2. What required actions can a policy demand?
3. What counts as evidence that a requirement was satisfied?
4. Where should policy state live?
5. How should guided vs required mode be represented in config?
6. When should the runner block, allow, or allow-with-warning?
7. How should host adapters interact with the runner?
8. What should the MCP surface look like, if added later?

## Proposed Architecture Areas
### 1. Policy engine
Evaluate rules for a given trigger and context, then return:
- `allow`
- `allow_with_recommendation`
- `require_action`
- `block`

### 2. Evidence store
Track whether required steps have already occurred for the current task/session/workflow, for example:
- recall performed
- checkpoint created
- writeback stored
- skip reason recorded

### 3. Integration layer
Allow the policy engine to be called from:
- wrapper CLI commands
- git hooks
- editor or agent integrations
- CI checks
- future MCP handlers

## Proposed v1 Trigger Set
Candidate triggers:
- `on_task_start`
- `on_git_commit`
- `on_pr_open`
- `on_release`
- `on_migration`
- `on_review`

The design should recommend which subset belongs in v1 and why.

## Proposed v1 Required Actions
Candidate required actions:
- `recall`
- `writeback`
- `checkpoint`
- `skip_reason`
- `scope_selected`
- `classification_selected`

The design should define which of these are first-class and how they are verified.

## Proposed Config Shape
The plan should propose a repository-local config format, likely under a path such as `.mnemix/policy.toml`, including:

- rule definition
- trigger matching
- context filters
- guided vs required mode
- skip permissions
- enforcement behavior
- default scopes or adapter policies

The config example should be concrete and human-editable.

## Proposed Evaluation Flow
The plan should define a concrete runtime flow:

1. Host emits a trigger.
2. Policy runner builds an evaluation context.
3. Matching rules are loaded from config.
4. Required actions are derived.
5. Evidence is checked.
6. A decision is returned with explanations.
7. The host either continues, prompts, or blocks.

## Coding-Agent-First Requirements
Because coding agents are the first-class host, the plan should explicitly cover:
- commit-time writeback policy
- repo/workspace/session/task scope policy
- explicit low-signal skip behavior
- checkpoint-before-risky-change behavior
- how policy runner decisions compose with `CodingAgentAdapter`

## MCP Positioning
The design should clarify whether MCP is:
- the enforcement layer
- the transport/API layer
- optional for v1

The current expectation is that MCP should be treated as an optional interoperability surface, while enforcement remains host-side via wrappers, hooks, or orchestration. The plan should either confirm or reject that approach with reasoning.

## Deliverables
The completed design should produce:
- an architecture summary
- a proposed config format
- a trigger/action/evidence model
- a CLI/API proposal
- an MCP integration recommendation
- a phased implementation roadmap
- risk and tradeoff notes

## Acceptance Criteria
This planning task is complete when:
- the policy runner architecture is documented clearly enough to implement without major redesign
- guided and required modes are both modeled explicitly
- commit-time enforcement is covered concretely
- evidence tracking and skip-reason behavior are defined
- the relationship between the policy runner, adapters, CLI, hooks, and MCP is explicit
- the rollout plan is phased and realistic

## Verification
Verification for this planning task should include:
- a written review against current Mnemix adapter and Python client boundaries
- confirmation that the design keeps storage semantics separate from policy semantics
- confirmation that the design can support both guided and strict workflows without forcing low-signal memory writes
result: Drafted policy runner technical design in docs/policy-runner-design.md. Covered architecture, trigger/action/evidence model, local config and evidence files, CLI/API proposal, coding-agent-first defaults, MCP positioning, phased rollout, and risks. Verification: reviewed against python/mnemix/client.py, adapters/coding_agent_adapter.py, docs_site/src/guide/host-adapters.md, and docs/mnemix-coding-guidelines.md to keep policy above adapters and storage.

name: Set up AI context files for coding agents
description: Create and standardize the repository-local AI context files that coding agents should use while working on Mnemix. Scope: decide which agent-facing context files should exist (for example AGENTS.md plus any additional repo-local context/instruction files needed for common coding agents), define the shared project context that should be duplicated or referenced across them, and ensure the contents align with docs/mnemix-plan-v3.md, docs/mnemix-roadmap.md, docs/lancedb-rust-sdk-agent-guide.md, and docs/mnemix-coding-guidelines.md. Include: project purpose, architecture boundaries, active milestones, validation expectations, Dex workflow expectations, and any agent-specific notes needed to keep work consistent. Acceptance criteria: the required agent context files are identified and created, they are internally consistent, they reference the canonical project docs, and they are suitable for use by coding agents working in this repo without re-discovery of core project context.
result: Set up repo-local AI context for Mnemix using AI DX Toolkit with a lean Rust-library-focused configuration. Disabled the optional temporal/lance-context add-on, disabled the compliance agent and compliance instructions, generated .ai/ output, rewrote the active AGENTS/instructions/context files to match the current single-repo planning/scaffold state, pruned N/A API/auth/RBAC prompts and context files, and aligned the updater docs with the reduced context set. Verification: toolkit config validated, generation completed successfully, representative .ai files report no errors, and active .ai content no longer references compliance-standards or the removed API/auth/RBAC context files.

name: Add embedding write-path support
description: Implement the next vector retrieval slice in the codex/vector-retrieval worktree. Scope: persist actual embeddings in the LanceDB memories table on write and via applied backfill when a runtime embedding provider is attached, while keeping retrieval behavior unchanged. Acceptance criteria: vector-enabled stores can store embedding values, plan/apply backfill updates persisted embedding fields, and tests cover write-path persistence and backfill application without yet enabling semantic search.
result: Implemented embedding write-path persistence in mnemix-lancedb. Added schema v3 with a persisted embedding list column while preserving legacy and pre-embedding v2 store compatibility, auto-embed-on-write persistence for provider-backed stores, and applied backfill writes that update stored embedding fields. Verified with cargo fmt --all, cargo test -p mnemix-lancedb --lib, and cargo test -p mnemix-core -p mnemix-lancedb -p mnemix-cli.

name: Validate baseline and docs
description: Finish Milestone 0 by validating the workspace baseline and recording the milestone outcome. Scope: run the baseline checks, confirm the scaffold builds, ensure docs and metadata are coherent, and update the Dex task result with verification notes when the milestone is done. Acceptance criteria: fmt/clippy/test/doc checks are runnable, the placeholder workspace is healthy, and milestone verification is clearly captured for later sessions.
result: Validated the workspace baseline and documentation scaffold. Generated Cargo.lock, formatted the workspace, and ran cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings, cargo test --workspace, cargo doc --workspace --no-deps, and cargo deny check successfully. Also installed the missing Rust toolchain path via rustup stable and installed cargo-deny so the baseline checks are runnable locally. Verification: all baseline checks passed successfully on the scaffolded workspace.

name: Add vector enablement and backfill flows
description: Implement the next vector retrieval slice in the codex/vector-retrieval worktree. Scope: add explicit backend APIs and CLI flows to enable vectors, persist vector settings, and run embedding backfill safely without enabling semantic retrieval yet. Acceptance criteria: vector settings can be enabled intentionally, invalid enablement states are rejected, backfill-safe plumbing exists behind explicit commands or requests, and the new flows are covered by tests.
result: Implemented explicit vector enablement and backfill flows in the codex/vector-retrieval worktree. Backend changes: added VectorEnableRequest, EmbeddingBackfillRequest, and EmbeddingBackfillResult in mnemix-lancedb; added LanceDbBackend::enable_vectors() to persist validated vector settings for schema-v2 stores; added LanceDbBackend::backfill_embeddings() with safe plan-mode behavior and explicit apply-mode gating that currently requires a runtime embedding provider and still reports write-path backfill as not implemented. CLI changes: added , , and  commands to the Mnemix CLI;  reports current settings and runtime capability,  persists model/dimension settings, and  runs a plan-only count by default. Tests: added backend coverage for enablement persistence, legacy-store rejection, backfill planning, and apply-mode provider requirements; added CLI integration coverage for the new vector commands and their JSON status shape. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check; cargo test -p mnemix-core -p mnemix-lancedb -p mnemix-cli all passed.

name: Milestone 3: Human-first CLI MVP
description: Implement Milestone 3 from docs/mnemix-roadmap.md. Scope: create the CLI commands for init, remember, search, show, pins, history, checkpoint, versions, stats, export, import, and JSON output mode. Keep human-readable output strong and snapshot-testable. Follow the roadmap's guidance to separate command execution from output rendering and keep binary-level error aggregation out of the library layers. Acceptance criteria: users can inspect stored memory, timestamps, and checkpoints from the terminal, commands support predictable human-readable output, machine-readable output is stable enough for adapter work, and snapshot tests protect output regressions.
result: Reconciled during post-merge cleanup: Milestone 3 had already landed and is marked complete to align the task file with the merged CLI work.

name: Add semantic candidate retrieval
description: Implement the next vector retrieval slice in the codex/vector-retrieval worktree. Scope: add semantic and hybrid candidate generation in mnemix-lancedb using persisted embeddings and the runtime embedding provider, while preserving lexical-only defaults and existing recall layering. Acceptance criteria: SearchQuery and RecallQuery retrieval modes are honored by the LanceDB backend, semantic-only and hybrid retrieval work when vectors/providers are available, lexical behavior remains unchanged by default, and tests cover search/recall semantic paths and capability advertising.
result: Implemented additive semantic and hybrid retrieval in mnemix-lancedb using persisted embeddings and runtime embedding providers, while keeping lexical-only behavior as the default. SearchQuery and RecallQuery now honor RetrievalMode in the LanceDB backend, backend capability advertising includes semantic/hybrid support when runtime conditions are met, recall explanations distinguish semantic-only vs hybrid matches, and backend tests cover semantic search, hybrid recall, and capability exposure. Verified with cargo fmt --all, cargo test -p mnemix-lancedb --lib, and cargo test -p mnemix-core -p mnemix-lancedb -p mnemix-cli.

name: Milestone 4: Progressive disclosure and pinning semantics
description: Implement Milestone 4 from docs/mnemix-roadmap.md. Scope: add explicit pin/unpin support, pinned-context retrieval, summary-first recall, archival expansion, retrieval explanation metadata, and ranking heuristics for recency, importance, and pinned state. Keep pinned vs archival memory first-class, as defined in docs/mnemix-plan-v3.md. Acceptance criteria: recall returns layered results rather than a flat list, pinned context is consistently favored but bounded, summaries and archival items are distinguishable, and surfaced items include explanation metadata suitable for inspection.
result: Reconciled during post-merge cleanup: Milestone 4 had already landed and is marked complete so the Dex chain is consistent again.

name: Distill agent coding guidelines
description: Review the full docs/coding-guidlines source material, extract only the project-relevant rules for Mnemix, create a strict distilled guideline document for agents, update references to point to the distilled document, and remove the temporary upstream guideline copies before the PR merges. Acceptance criteria: a single distilled guideline document remains as the source of truth, AGENTS.md and planning docs reference it where appropriate, and the temporary coding-guidlines contents are removed from the repo.
result: Reviewed the temporary imported coding guideline sources and distilled the project-relevant rules into docs/coding-guidlines/mnemix-agent-guidelines.md. The distilled guide now captures strict Mnemix-specific rules for architecture boundaries, Rust API design, error handling, testing, docs, dependency hygiene, CI discipline, performance/ownership, Python binding boundaries, and LanceDB leakage prevention. Updated AGENTS.md, docs/mnemix-roadmap.md, docs/repo-scaffold-spec.md, and the Milestone 1 Dex task to point at the distilled guide. Removed the temporary imported contents from docs/coding-guidlines so only the distilled guideline file remains. Verification: references now point to the new file, docs validation shows no errors, and the directory now contains only docs/coding-guidlines/mnemix-agent-guidelines.md.

name: Add vector-capable LanceDB schema scaffolding
description: Implement the next vector retrieval slice in mnemix-lancedb. Scope: add vector-capable schema and store metadata scaffolding for optional embeddings, keep existing lexical behavior unchanged, and preserve compatibility for existing stores. Acceptance criteria: the backend can initialize stores with nullable embedding columns and vector config metadata, old stores continue to open, and tests cover the schema/version behavior without yet enabling semantic search.
result: Implemented the vector-schema scaffolding slice in mnemix-lancedb while preserving current lexical behavior. Changes: bumped new-store schema version to 2; added a dedicated vector_config table with default disabled settings and optional embedding metadata fields; added version-aware memory row encoding so new stores write the v2 row shape while legacy v1 stores remain readable and writable; added nullable embedding metadata columns to the v2 memories schema; updated the CLI flow test to expect schema version 2 for freshly initialized stores. Compatibility: existing stores continue to open at schema version 1, receive the new vector_config table automatically, and keep using the legacy memory row shape for remember/search flows. Verification: cargo fmt --all --check; cargo test -p mnemix-core -p mnemix-lancedb -p mnemix-cli all passed.
